title: Set Up a Production Linux Server
description: Configure a Linux server from scratch for hosting web applications. Covers SSH hardening, firewall setup, Nginx reverse proxy, SSL certificates, and basic monitoring.

steps.title: Initial Server Setup
steps.description:
Provision a fresh Linux server and perform the essential first steps.

1. Spin up a VPS with Ubuntu 22.04+ (e.g., DigitalOcean, Linode, or Hetzner)
2. Log in as root via SSH for the first time
3. Update all packages with `apt update && apt upgrade -y`
4. Set the hostname and timezone (`hostnamectl set-hostname`, `timedatectl set-timezone`)
5. Create a non-root user with sudo privileges (`adduser deploy && usermod -aG sudo deploy`)
6. Verify you can log in as the new user and run sudo commands
checkpoints: VPS is provisioned and accessible via SSH
All system packages are updated
Hostname and timezone are configured
Non-root user with sudo access is created
Able to SSH in as the new user and run sudo

steps.title: SSH Hardening
steps.description:
Secure SSH access to prevent unauthorized login attempts.

1. Generate an SSH key pair on your local machine if you don't have one (`ssh-keygen -t ed25519`)
2. Copy your public key to the server (`ssh-copy-id deploy@your-server-ip`)
3. Edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` to disable root login (`PermitRootLogin no`)
4. Disable password authentication (`PasswordAuthentication no`)
5. Change the default SSH port to a non-standard port (e.g., 2222)
6. Restart the SSH service and verify you can still connect with your key
checkpoints: SSH key-based authentication is working
Root login via SSH is disabled
Password authentication is disabled
SSH port changed from default 22
SSH service restarts cleanly and connection works on new port

steps.title: Firewall Configuration
steps.description:
Set up UFW to control inbound and outbound traffic.

1. Install and enable UFW (`apt install ufw`)
2. Set the default policies: deny incoming, allow outgoing
3. Allow your custom SSH port (`ufw allow 2222/tcp`)
4. Allow HTTP (80) and HTTPS (443) traffic
5. Enable the firewall (`ufw enable`) and verify the rules with `ufw status verbose`
6. Optionally install and configure Fail2Ban to block brute-force attempts
checkpoints: UFW is installed and enabled
Default policy denies incoming traffic
SSH, HTTP, and HTTPS ports are allowed
ufw status shows correct rules
Fail2Ban is installed and monitoring SSH (optional but recommended)

steps.title: Install and Configure Nginx
steps.description:
Set up Nginx as a reverse proxy for your web application.

1. Install Nginx (`apt install nginx`)
2. Verify Nginx is running by visiting your server's IP in a browser
3. Create a new server block config in `/etc/nginx/sites-available/your-app`
4. Configure it as a reverse proxy to `localhost:3000` (or your app's port)
5. Enable the site by symlinking to `sites-enabled` and remove the default site
6. Test the config (`nginx -t`) and reload Nginx
7. Verify your proxy works by running a test app on the backend port
checkpoints: Nginx is installed and running
Custom server block config exists in sites-available
Config proxies requests to the backend application port
Site is enabled via symlink in sites-enabled
nginx -t passes without errors
Requests to the server IP are proxied to the backend

steps.title: SSL/TLS with Let's Encrypt
steps.description:
Secure your server with a free SSL certificate from Let's Encrypt.

1. Point a domain name to your server's IP address (add an A record in DNS)
2. Install Certbot and the Nginx plugin (`apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx`)
3. Obtain a certificate (`certbot --nginx -d yourdomain.com`)
4. Verify the certificate is installed by visiting `https://yourdomain.com`
5. Check that HTTP automatically redirects to HTTPS
6. Verify auto-renewal is set up (`certbot renew --dry-run`)
checkpoints: Domain DNS A record points to server IP
Certbot is installed with the Nginx plugin
SSL certificate is obtained and applied
Site is accessible over HTTPS
HTTP to HTTPS redirect is working
Certificate auto-renewal dry run succeeds

steps.title: Deploy a Web Application
steps.description:
Deploy an actual application and manage it with a process manager.

1. Install Node.js via nvm or the NodeSource repository
2. Clone your application repository to `/var/www/your-app`
3. Install dependencies and build the app
4. Install PM2 globally (`npm install -g pm2`) to manage the process
5. Start your app with PM2 (`pm2 start index.js --name your-app`)
6. Configure PM2 to start on boot (`pm2 startup && pm2 save`)
7. Verify the app is accessible through your domain over HTTPS
checkpoints: Node.js is installed on the server
Application code is deployed to /var/www/your-app
Dependencies are installed and app builds successfully
PM2 is running the application process
PM2 is configured to auto-start on reboot
Application is accessible via the domain over HTTPS

steps.title: Monitoring and Logging
steps.description:
Set up basic monitoring so you know when something goes wrong.

1. Configure Nginx access and error log rotation with `logrotate`
2. Install and configure a monitoring tool (e.g., Netdata for real-time metrics)
3. Set up `pm2 monit` to watch application CPU and memory usage
4. Configure unattended security updates (`apt install unattended-upgrades`)
5. Set up a simple uptime check (e.g., UptimeRobot or a cron-based curl check)
6. Review logs with `journalctl` and verify log output from your app and Nginx
checkpoints: Log rotation is configured for Nginx logs
Monitoring dashboard is accessible (Netdata or similar)
PM2 shows application metrics
Unattended security upgrades are enabled
Uptime monitoring is configured and reporting
Logs are accessible via journalctl

steps.title: Backup and Disaster Recovery
steps.description:
Ensure your server and data can be restored if something goes wrong.

1. Write a backup script that archives your application directory and database (if any)
2. Schedule the backup with a cron job (e.g., daily at 2 AM)
3. Configure the script to upload backups to a remote location (e.g., S3, Backblaze B2, or another server via rsync)
4. Retain only the last 7 daily backups to save space
5. Test a full restore: spin up a new server, restore from backup, and verify the app runs
6. Document your disaster recovery procedure
checkpoints: Backup script exists and runs without errors
Cron job is scheduled for daily backups
Backups are uploaded to a remote/off-server location
Old backups are cleaned up automatically
Full restore tested successfully on a fresh server
Disaster recovery steps are documented
